The rebuilding has stalled. For years, the foundation of the temple has sat incomplete—a monument to good intentions interrupted by opposition and fear. Then God acts, not with dramatic force but through His prophets Haggai and Zechariah. The message is simple yet terrifying: rise up and build. And remarkably, the people obey. Zerubbabel and Jeshua respond immediately, rallying the workers to resume the work that cost them so much to begin. This is what it looks like when God stirs His people from complacency. Not everyone will understand it. When Tattenai the governor arrives with officials demanding authorization, he sees only what appears to be illegal construction. He does not see what the text reveals: the eye of their God was upon the elders of the Jews. God was watching. God was present. God was actively protecting the work even when the workers could not see how the story would end.

Notice what happens in this moment of interrogation. The Jews do not defend themselves with political cleverness or careful diplomacy. Instead, they testify. They identify themselves first and foremost as servants of the God of heaven and earth, not as subjects of Babylon or residents seeking permission. They explain that they are rebuilding what was destroyed, that their own fathers angered God and were judged for it, and that King Cyrus himself—the very ruler now on the throne—had issued a decree authorizing this work. There is no pleading, no wheedling. There is only honesty about who they are, what they're doing, and why. The officials respond not with violence but with bureaucracy: a letter to the king asking for verification. What looks like continued obstruction is actually the machinery through which God will provide confirmation of His will.

This chapter confronts us with a hard truth about living faithfully: obedience does not guarantee smooth sailing. The moment the workers move forward in faith, resistance appears. Opposition is not a sign that you've missed God's will—it can be the very proof that you're doing exactly what God asked. Yet notice also that God's eye was upon them. The phrase appears quietly in verse 5, almost parenthetically, yet it carries the full weight of divine sovereignty. God was not waiting in heaven to see if the Jews could figure out how to overcome this problem. He was there, watching, ensuring that the work would not be stopped but would instead be tested and verified. The king's archives would confirm what Cyrus had decreed. God's will would be vindicated not through the Jews' persuasive arguments but through the preserved record of a pagan king's ancient command. Sometimes faith means doing the work and trusting that God has already prepared the way forward, even when you cannot yet see it.
